windows.summary_v2: A maker uses a heat gun and a thin metal probe to remelt and smooth the tops of two pale-yellow soy candles, popping surface bubbles before letting them set.

seconds_v2.desc: Top-down on two pale-yellow soy candles in clear glass tumblers on a wood table; a thin metal probe enters from upper-left toward the left candle, which has an upright wooden wick under a silver centering bar.
The probe tip presses into the left candle's still-glossy surface near the wooden wick, working at the soft wax to pop bubbles after a heat-gun pass.
Probe withdraws to the left; both candle tops look freshly remelted and shiny, wooden wicks centered under their metal wick bars.
Static top-down view of the two setting candles; the cream-yellow wax surfaces stay wet-looking and smooth around each wick.
Both candles continue to cool; a faint sheen lingers near the left wick where the bubbles were smoothed out.
Unchanged framing; the molten top layer of each candle remains liquid-smooth and reflective as it begins to level.
The two candles sit untouched, surfaces slowly losing their wettest shine while the wooden wicks stay upright.
Final hold on both pale-yellow candles solidifying side by side, smooth tops with centered wooden wicks, no tool in frame.

windows.transcript: All right. I think it's tricky to see on the camera about there is a bubble inside. So I'm just going to go ahead and smash that bubble out.
windows.embed_text_v2: pressing a metal spoon into a single cream soy candle surface to smash out a bubble near the wooden wick, clear glass tumbler, wick centering bars, wood table, hand visible
windows.summary_v2: The maker presses a long metal spoon into the setting surface of a single cream soy candle to smash out a hidden bubble near the wick.

seconds_v2.desc: Top-down on a single cream soy candle in a clear glass tumbler centered on a wood table; two metal wick-centering bars rest behind it and a wooden wick stands upright in the smooth wax.
A thin reflection of a tool sweeps in from the left as the maker moves a long metal spoon toward the candle; the cream surface is matte and mostly set.
A long metal spoon enters from the left and its tip reaches the wax surface beside the wooden wick.
Fingertips appear at the left edge gripping the spoon; its bowl presses into the wax right at the base of the wick to smash out a trapped bubble.
The spoon tip pushes and drags through the soft surface near the wick, working the wax to release the bubble underneath.
Continued pressing with the spoon, leaving a slight glossy depression in the wax around the wooden wick.
The spoon stirs the disturbed wax near the wick, smoothing the spot where the bubble was popped.
Final second: the spoon eases back, leaving a smoothed, faintly swirled patch around the centered wooden wick of the cream candle.
